entities.name: jñāna
entities.cite: cognition arises naturally, needing no instruction

entities.name: śāstras
entities.cite: there would be no purpose whatever in the sastras

entities.name: amnāya
entities.cite: amnayo nibandhanam: tradition is knowledge's ground

entities.name: artha
entities.cite: there would be no purpose whatever in the sastras

axioms.name: hetutvam
axioms.cite: grant what the tradition holds: that dharma conditions knowing — the knower's moral-ritual formation is causally upstream of his cognitive reach

axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: since dharma is not established by unaided reason (1.30), amnayo nibandhanam: tradition is knowledge's ground

axioms.name: qualifies_knower
axioms.cite: disciplines do not merely inform; they *qualify the knower*

denials.name: svabhavika
denials.cite: Either the sastras are idle (which practice refutes) or they are load-bearing via dharma (which makes agama foundational)

denials.name: artha_rahita
denials.cite: then the sastras are pointless; yet no party to the debate, least of all the naturalist writing books, treats them so

claims.name: hetutvam
claims.cite: if dharma is the cause of knowledge

rejected_readings.label: innate_knowledge
rejected_readings.rendering: Knowledge is innate and arises naturally without instruction or tradition.
rejected_readings.why: Asserts that jñāna is svābhāvika (naturally given), which leads to the false consequence that śāstras are purposeless. The commentary uses this as a reductio: no one, especially not rationalist philosophers, actually treats scriptures as useless.

rejected_readings.label: rational_sufficiency
rejected_readings.rendering: Knowledge can be established by reason alone, without the need for moral-ritual formation.
rejected_readings.why: Denies that dharma is causally necessary for knowledge. It treats jñāna as self-standing, independent of the knower's moral-ritual preparation. The commentary explicitly rejects this: 'the knower's moral-ritual formation is causally upstream of his cognitive reach'.

rejected_readings.label: scriptures_descriptive_only
rejected_readings.rendering: The scriptures serve only to describe knowledge that is already established by reason, not to ground it.
rejected_readings.why: Denies that amnāya is the ground (nibandhanam) of jñāna, treating it as merely descriptive rather than foundational. The commentary is explicit: 'since dharma is not established by unaided reason, amnayo nibandhanam: tradition is knowledge's ground'.

notes: The verse formalizes a strategic dilemma (dilemma/dvayasrama): the naturalist position (svabhavika-vada) either trivializes all pedagogy via reductio, or concedes that dharma and amnaya are foundational to knowledge. The contract encodes the endorsed horn: dharma (moral-ritual formation) conditions the knower, amnaya (transmitted teaching) grounds knowledge. Contested: whether the second pada should be read as Bhartrhari's endorsement or continued presentation of alternatives; contract follows the prima facie reading where the conditional is endorsed.
